fact: January 7 is exactly one week after New Year's Day, which means it's the first Monday of the year about 14% of the time!
In the Northern Hemisphere, this is when many animals are at their fluffiest - their winter coats are at maximum thickness to survive the coldest weeks ahead.
The sun rises about 1 minute later each day around January 7 in most places, but sunsets actually start getting later too - you gain evening light faster than you lose morning light!
January 7 falls smack in the middle of Capricorn season, when people born under this zodiac sign are supposedly the most stubborn and goal-focused.
Tree sap moves so slowly on January 7 in cold climates that maple trees are basically frozen solid inside - like nature's popsicles!
This date is roughly when Earth reaches its closest point to the sun each year, called perihelion - we're actually 3 million miles closer to the sun than in summer!
In Antarctica, January 7 brings some of the warmest weather of the year, with temperatures that might actually climb above freezing for the first time in months.
Many hibernating animals like bears and groundhogs are in their deepest sleep phase around this time - their heart rates drop to just 8 beats per minute!
January 7 is when ice on ponds and lakes in cold regions becomes thick enough for ice fishing - the ice needs to be at least 4 inches thick to be safe.
The moon phase on January 7 changes every year, but when it's a full moon on this date, it appears extra bright because of all the snow reflecting moonlight back up into the sky.
In many northern cities, January 7 marks the time when your breath becomes visible in the air almost every morning - the 'frost breath' season is in full swing.
Evergreen trees like pine and spruce actually smell stronger around January 7 because the cold air makes their oils more concentrated - it's like nature's air freshener!
